title: PrioSync
description: I built PrioSync after feeling the daily friction of staring at a huge task list and still not knowing what to do first.

content.text: I did not start PrioSync because I wanted another productivity app. I started it because my own task list kept stealing time from actual work.
I had urgent tasks, important tasks, and blocked tasks all mixed together. On paper it looked organized, but in practice I kept choosing the wrong next step.

So I built a weighted priority engine that scores urgency, importance, deadline pressure, and task ease. Instead of manually tagging everything as high, the system calculates what deserves attention now.
The turning point was replacing subjective labels with objective scoring.
High score did not always mean executable work. Dependencies kept breaking the flow, so I implemented DAG logic with cycle detection and topological sorting to produce valid order.
The worst bug was a smart-looking recommendation for a task I could not even start.
I also fixed stale multi-tab behavior using browser events and localStorage sync signals, then deduped refresh calls so changes appeared quickly without noisy API traffic.
PrioSync taught me that productivity UX is mostly trust. If the next-task signal is consistently right, users stop debating the list and start shipping work.

content.suggestion: Task priorities automatically reordering as a deadline approaches and dependency status changes
